speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five-- Mr. Hirsch, I find it very interesting that you decided to continue your education at an American university. And when you were in South Bend, you told me that you enrolled in Notre Dame. Could you please tell me a little bit about that experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when we had English in high school, and I later took some technical English at university, we actually learned the King's English. And I was out of place in a way in the surroundings that I had to work. So I first started out in New Brunswick in public school English for foreigners. I quickly breezed through that. Then I went to Rutgers, and I took some additional courses in engineering. And when I later transferred onto the job in South Bend, I took the opportunity to study additional engineering courses as well as cost accounting, I must admit, at Notre Dame-- evening courses. And I found it very fulfilling that I was continuing a little bit in the academic atmosphere, even though I had my so-called engineering degree-- not the piece of paper, but 4 and 1/2 years of engineering behind me. And I, I felt very much at home in that atmosphere, and I really enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the atmosphere of the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the university, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it affect you in any way that Notre Dame is, is a Catholic institution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the instructors wore the garb, but others didn't. And it didn't seem to come through.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you felt no discrimination.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel any discrimination, no. I had to be accepted, which I was after I gave them my background. The same at Rutgers-- I was accepted for whatever courses I chose to take. And I felt no discrimination there. The only discrimination was on the job by those who were from the corn belt. Indiana is well known for-- I wouldn't say being backwards, but traditional views. And they were all good Protestants.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking now 1943, perhaps, or '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Indiana '43, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, you packed up your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that I-- when I, when I finally succumbed to my wife's pleadings again, I got a job in Chicago with a company who had a New York office and put me in charge of that office as supervising engineer for the Eastern Seaboard. I traveled extensively as well with the various agents, agents from Buffalo down to Baltimore, selling machinery. But at the war's end, the firm collapsed. So I had to find another job. Oh, I should mention that one of the triggering reasons that I decided to move to New York was that the injuries I sustained in my car accident-- broken jaws, et cetera-- nobody wanted to work on it in Chicago. They felt it was a very complicated operation. And so in New York, I had interviews. And there was a surgeon who expressed willingness to undertake it. And he did with reasonable success.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this accident? Where did you have this? In South Bend?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Elizabeth, New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, and so I got this other job in New York. But all the time, I had this idea of going in business for myself. And I finally did. I started my own engineering company and stayed with it for 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have two children. Can you tell us about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sons. My oldest son would have made a wonderful engineer, but when he saw me struggle in work, he said, I want nothing to do with specialties. I have to peddle a product.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Howard, Howard David Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: --David after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944-- May '44. And Howard got his degree from Columbia and Master Business Administration, worked during the summers for Kodak and other companies, and got a-- was interviewed by Proctor and Gamble, and got a job in Cincinnati, which is, I must say, quite a feat. Because Proctor and Gamble, in those days, and still today is considered the master marketer and merchandiser the United States there, like General Motors in engineering. And he married in New York. And unfortunately, the marriage went to pieces in Cincinnati. And he moved back to New York and got a job with Carter-Wallace, became a product manager, finally got a corner office with a window. And there was another fellow, an immigrant from Cuba, who had an office next door. And he also was product manager. He had also run through the ranks. One time, Howard was handling Arrid, and this chap was handling Oil of Olay. So somehow the two mixed-- used together-- when used together. And they decided to go strike out in business for themselves. And it didn't take long that they really made the grade, moved from a small place up in the Bronx, where they bought a almost defunct business, expanded it, and Howard was driving the hi-lo-- forklift to you uninitiated-- and moved to New Jersey, and built up a sizable business, and sold it to the Japanese. And Howard is still president and chief executive officer of the Japanese company here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tsumura International. One of the sons of the owner of Tsumura in Japan, who is an old gentlemen-- older than I. He's very old-- is here the chairman of the board. But other than that, the management is strictly in the hands of Howard, Al-- Alfonso Lopez-- and a third partner, Levine, or Levine as he likes to pronounce it, who run the company now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- your son lives in New Jersey now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in New Jersey. Lives in Basking Ridge. He has three sons. My son in his second marriage is married to Pamela. Pamela Parish was her maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They've been married now 20 years-- in the '50s. And-- '60s, I should say. I'm sorry. And they have three children-- Brad, Brian, and Bradley.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful boys. One just turned 17. I went with him to get the driver's license. The other one's 8 and 1/2. The little one will be 11. And we're very close with these boys. I taught them-- I taught Howard how to swim, to play ping pong, and ride a bike. And I taught my grandchildren the same thing, which makes for a nice continuity in life. I think that's something that I really enjoy. My youngest son, Kenneth-- Kenneth William after my mother-- all his life he was not a magnificent student. But he showed tremendous aptitude for music. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: --as a young lad--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in '48, four years younger than Howard. As a young, young boy, maybe six or seven, he already had absolute pitch and was accepted by Julliard in a very simple test. I was there with him. And he went through Julliard. And then he studied arranging and composing at Queens College, which incidentally has an outstanding music school. And he first worked a little bit in New York. But then he worked for Motown, for two contracts. And then he decided--

speaker: interviewer
text: Motown Records-- Motown Records?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motown, yes. And he got married. He married a girl from Cleveland. And they moved to LA. And he lives in LA. He works for-- well, now, he's between jobs. But the music business is a very difficult business. He worked up to-- he still does until the contract is up-- for Warner/Chappell Music Company, a division of Warner. And he has a condominium in the city and a little house up in the mountains-- Lake Arrowhead. And they had no children. His lifestyle is different from my son's lifestyle, which is very programmed and regulated, being in business, even though he travels a lot. His lifestyle is more that of an artist. In the job that he is in, he doesn't follow any regular hours. He makes his own time as long as it brings in a few scribbled notes on lined paper. That's his job. And it's a difficult business, though, I want to stress. He has had some hits. But it's extremely difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to ask you, Mr. Hirsch. What made you decide to share your story with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren have been prodding me for the longest time to tell them what happened, on and off. And I just-- I never burdened my two sons with all the baggage that Jews usually carry. Because it is not only one's own experience, it is a group experience that we carry. There's no question about it. That is why Jews gather to help other Jews. That's why I got soup at the HIAS. They didn't know me-- couldn't care less. But there was another Jew that came-- and we wanted to keep on the straight and narrow. You shouldn't wind up going through garbage cans. And so my grandchildren have been asking me for the longest time to put this on a tape. And I just-- I'm busy. I'm still working. I'm still-- I just completed the design of the building of some [INAUDIBLE] machine. And so I never really find the time to sit down and do and collect my thoughts. And frankly speaking, this was like an opportunity to do the very thing that I had wanted to do but never really worked up enough steam to do. And especially when I was told that I may get a tape, that was really an inducement. I didn't know it would be that many reels, but it really an inducement to pass this on to them, this story. And they are from a mixed marriage. They're not being brought up in any religious way. They're ambivalent about it. They say they have the best of both worlds. They celebrate Hanukkah in our home and Christmas in their home. And I've never-- we were never that religious. But it's interesting how the-- how things twist you around. And now that we have this ambivalence, we do celebrate Hanukkah with gift giving. And we do celebrate Passover with a meal and they go predominantly for the matzo balls. The Haggadah is too long for them. Can't speak Hebrew anyway. And we also celebrate Christmas with them and Easter in their home. My daughter puts a beautiful Christmas tree. And the last Hanukkah we were there, my son surprised us with an old menorah that I never knew I had given him years ago. So they're trying to do the best in circumstances, which at times can become difficult. I know a racial intermarriage may be tougher, but a religious intermarriage also has its problems. And I know when I was a young lad and I was idealistic, I thought to myself, makes no difference if I marry a Gentile girl. Everything will be wonderful. And it can be. But it takes a lot of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to ask you, Mr. Hirsch. For anyone who views this tape, is there a message? You've been through so much in your life. Is there a message you would like to convey to anyone who views this tape, including your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, predominantly my grandchildren. My sons know me well enough. They-- my older son and I, we have a wonderful relationship. As a matter of fact, in this huge office that their company occupies, I have a small office. In one of their warehouses, I have a corner for my inventory. So we have a very good relationship. We see each other regularly-- not only in the office almost every day, but also at home. So for my grandchildren, however, which I have to look forward to a different world that is arising-- entirely different world than I've been through, and even a different world than my sons went through. The world is becoming smaller. It is becoming denser. And the problems that come with it are becoming more grave. And I would tell them, number one, not to forget both halves of their backgrounds, both halves. Because it is important for them to understand their mother's upbringing and thoughts and also their father's upbringing and thoughts. And under the circumstances, what I can ask them to be is decent human beings, who are considerate of other people, and who will try to be gentle and kind, even at times it takes an effort to be so, toward their fellow man. Nothing will ever be perfect in our lives, in our children's lives, and in their lives. But it isn't really perfection that one has to strive for. It is to be decent. Lead a productive, decent life, and be considerate of their fellow man, not perpetuate this sort of inhumanity from man to man, which is really the problem. It isn't really whether it's Germans against Jews, or whether it's Arabs against Jews, or whether it's Greeks against Turks. It's this amazing hatred that is in all of us, and which, when permitted to come out, causes these tremendous upheavals and tragedy to human beings and families. I want them to keep that in mind and try to make sure that they don't succumb to that, whether it's on one side or on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hirsch, thank you very, very much for sharing your life experience with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. It was a pleasure. This photo is of myself, Charles Hirsch-- in those days Karl Hirsch. When I was 17 years old, there was this great advertising campaign in Vienna to offer these photographs at a good price. And so my brother and I went ahead, and we had these pictures taken. This is my brother Walter. At the time, he was 21. Picture was taken by the same photographer, of course. And he was quite a good looking chap-- looked a little bit like Fred Astaire, people said. This is a carte d'identite, actually an identity card for a student of the [GERMAN] in Wien, as the masthead says on this picture. The red stamp says Inlander, which means that he's an Austrian rather than a foreign student. And you had to carry this identification through your entire 4 and 1/2 years. On that picture, I am 19 years old. Another identity card issued already under the regime of the Nazis in 1938-- the photograph was taken a little earlier, probably when I was around 22. And you notice the swastikas to certify that it was authentic. This photo of my brother has relevance to the story that was taped. One might recall that he bought a bowler when the Nazis arrested us. That was high fashion in Vienna. One is tempted to say, think Yiddish but look British. And so it was the bowler that saved his cranium from being smashed by the Nazis. This is my mother, Wilhelmnie Hirsch, born Sabbath. She was born in Bohemia. And she had some very good education in Prague, in what was referred to as the [NON-ENGLISH], which is an upper women's school. She was excellent in math and in history and altogether a very caring mother. She was really a homebody, an excellent cook. My fondest memories go back to our times together. Picture of my father, Hugo David Hirsch. He was born in the German part of Bohemia called Sudetenland. The picture doesn't do him justice. In his younger years, his mustache was well waxed and taken care of, just like the old Austrian grenadiers. He had an excellent sense of humor and was extremely musical, including opera and symphonies. This is my older son Howard David Hirsch. The picture is taken in their garden. His wife Pamela-- her maiden name was Parrish-- and his three sons. There is Bradley on the left. Pam is holding the youngest, Douglas. And there is Brian on the right. This is my younger son, Kenneth William and his wife Karen. Her maiden name was Dolin. She hails from Cleveland. They were married just one week before Howard and Pam were married. So, call it a double header or a double loss, depending on who's talking. This is Kenny again. We met them-- him, rather-- at that time in Las Vegas. He was doing an introductory act, was the singer for the Smothers Brothers. And we had a wonderful time together. This is Howard's oldest son, Bradley Parrish Hirsch. He's now 18 and 1/2 and just about finished high school and getting ready for college. This is Brian Carl Hirsch. Brian is an excellent athlete. He is now 17. He just got his driver's license, and altogether very positive on life. This is Douglas Baxter Hirsch. He is really one smart fellow. I get along with him very well. He's excellent in school. And if he continues that way, he could make a good engineer. And this is my sister, Harriet Herta Hirsch. We all three came together to the United States, and she settled in Skokie, Illinois. Interestingly enough, each one of us had two sons. And Harriet, of course, has grandchildren, had grandchildren from her two sons. Unfortunately, she died prematurely in a car accident. She was a wonderful girl. This is my birth certificate from Vienna-- Karl Hirsch, the son of Hugo David and Wilhelmnie nee Sabbath Hirsch. I had the bar mitzvah in 1927 at the Vienna [NON-ENGLISH] temple or Stadttempel. You notice my Hebrew name is Yakov. My father's Hebrew name-- Ben-- David Ben Moshe. And my mother's Hebrew name was [HEBREW]. The rabbi was a famous Viennese and central European rabbi, Dr. Chajes. This is the certificate from the Viennese technical high school, lately Technical University. It's the government-- the first government exam. And I passed it with flying colors. It says in German, [GERMAN], which means with excellence. Down below it shows the date of my matura from the gymnasium, as my grandchildren keep kidding me, of 1933, and then 1936, the government-- first government exam at the technical university. This is a postcard I was permitted to send to my parents from Dachau. I was addressed as [GERMAN], in protective custody Jew. And it states my barracks as number 22 and second room. And on the left, they have the various restrictions that you can put on such a postcard or that the recipient can put in his letter as to how many lines, how it has to be arranged, and other details. This is already the second postcard. The first one was a form that was given to us that we just had to fill in. I'm feeling well. I'm being treated well. Everything is under control. On this card, I was permitted to go into some personal details, as long as they were not among the items prescribed by the authorities. This letter from my mother addressed to myself dates from December, 1940. She enumerates precisely what is needed to make my affidavit a valuable one. She states in the first sentence that the affidavit of Charles Hirsch is too weak and that I should try to procure additional affidavits from other people, and so on. I didn't make enough money yet-- you understand-- for them to accept my affidavit that I can support her. This is one of the many letters I received from my mother, each one getting more desperate with my father, who suffered a stroke after we children left. Getting worse, and altogether, the situation seeming more hopeless all the time. The backside of this letter shows the German Nazi stamp, believe it or not, from the Austrian army-- or rather, the German army-- who had time to examine and read each letter that was sent to America. A postcard from my mother in Theresienstadt addressed to the mother-in-law of my brother Walter in Vienna. She was Gentile, and so she continued to live there with her son, who was raised as a Catholic while her daughter was raised Jewish. This letter first had to go to Berlin to be then transferred to Vienna. And from there, Mrs. Berger was good enough to send it to us in America. In the very postcard, which my mother directed to Mrs. Berger, she writes that she-- from Theresienstadt, of course, in November the 6th, 1944-- that she's well, and she hopes to have some word from us, her children, if at all possible. After prolonged inquiries to various Jewish organizations in various cities, I finally, on August the 9th, '45, received this telegram from Prague-- Jewish community in Prague-- the famous Maislova [NON-ENGLISH], Maislova Street. According to inquiries, Wilhelmnie, alias Wilma, Hirsch did not yet return. And that was the final [INAUDIBLE].
